3: Anaerobic digestion is a microbiological process that is used in processing organic, biodegradable wastes and side streams into methane-rich biogas and nutrient-rich digestion residue, i.e. digestate. Biogas can be used as a renewable energy source for heat, combined heat and power (CHP) and/or for transportation fuel. Digestate, which contains all the nutrients of the treated materials,

can be used as a fertiliser directly or after further processing. Anaerobic digestion can play a role in achieving the international climate-related targets, such as the 40% reduction target of the EU's greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from 1990 levels by 2030 as well as an increase of 27% in the share of renewable energy and an improvement of 27% in energy efficiency (EC, 2014), while also making a positive contribution to meeting the targets of the Paris Agreement (UNFCCC Paris Agreement, 2015). In the agricultural sector, anaerobic digestion may reduce GHG emissions through enhanced use of manure (e.g. use as a substitute for raw material in energy production, decreased manure storage

time) and other agricultural by-products. In Finland, the annual emission reduction potential of biogas facilities on cattle farms alone has been estimated (by Winquist et al., 2015) at approxi- mately 9% of the national agricultural GHG emission reduction goal during in the 2005-2020 period (EU, 2009). Furthermore, anaer- obic digestion is promoted to increase closing of nutrient and material cycles in line with the EU circular economy package (EC, 2015). The Finnish strategy also focuses on directing actions to- wards improved life cycle carbon balance in bioenergy production.

The aforementioned targets and actions can be complicated to implement. To enable development of the steps with highest emissions (hot spots) in the management chains, more research on the energy and nutrient balances and environmental impacts of anaerobic digestion is required.

There are many different types of allocation methods (Hijazi 2016), and the choice of method is essential for outcomes and decision-making (Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2009). If allocation is car- ried out in accordance with societal values (e.g. in EU legislation digestate has been seen as a waste rather than as a valuable product), the emissions are allocated to the biogas and not to the digestate, which increases the environmental impacts of the energy consid- erably (Bachmaier, 2012; Hijazi et al., 2016). Physical allocation is considered to be the scientifically soundest option, as it is based on physical principles rather than societal values (Rehl et al., 2012). However, almost all emissions are allocated to biogas according to higher calorific values according to recommendations made in the EU Renewable Energy Directive (EU, 2009), thereby neglecting the nutrient-rich digestate as an integral co-product and failing to cover the entire carbon life cycle of digestion chains. The mass allocation method, which allocates emissions by weight, is easy to quantify, but it draws more attribution to the digestate as the by-product than on energy as the primary product (Hijazi et al., 2016). Mass allocation is not considered an appropriate way to allocate emissions between products, for example in the case of biogas and digestate production, because the purposes of the products are different. For this reason, mass allocation is usually only used for making comparisons (Hijazi et al., 2016; Bachmaier, 2012). The use of economic allocation method adds the complexity of price fluctuations over time and political influences through subsidies to the assessment of emissions (Hijazi et 2016; Rehl et al., 2012). However, allocating emissions for digestate may become more useful in the future as the markets for recycled fertilisers grow and the value of digestate increases relative to energy. Currently, only some studies have considered climate emissions of both energy and digestate utilization on field (Rehl and Müller, 2011; Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2015; Chiew et al., 2015), and some are more focused on the impact of e.g. feedstocks and choice of energy use (e.g. Berglund and Börjesson, 2006; Börjesson and Berglund, 2006; Pöschl et al., 2010; Uusitalo et al., 2014). Due to case-specificity, LCAs should also be conducted for different countries and conditions.

The aim of this study was to evaluate the climate emissions of the entire digestion chain, including both energy (biogas) and nu- trients (digestate), in a typical Finnish biogas plant processing pig slurry from several pig farms. The impacts were compared to those of using fossil energy and mineral fertilisers. Three different sce- narios for anaerobic digestion were assessed with co-digestion of pig slurry with separated solid fraction of pig slurry, ii) grass from uncultivated fields, or iii) food industry side streams. The whole life cycle of anaerobic digestion chains was considered, including feedstock procurement, digestion, storage of feedstocks and digestate, energy use (in CHP) and digestate fertiliser use. For the evaluation, the chain was split into two phases: 1) digestion of different feedstocks (from procurement to biogas plant gate) to biogas and digestate and 2) transport of fertilisers from the plant to spreading on the fields.

7: <header>2. Materials and methods </header>

This study was carried out using the LCA methodology set out in the global standards. In addition, the methodology described in EU Renewable Energy Directive (EU, 2009) was used where applicable. The assessed impact category was climate impact, including carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4) and dinitrogen monoxide (N20) emissions. Life Cycle Inventory results were characterised using factors suggested in EU Renewable Energy Directive (EU, 2009), i.e. CO = 1, CH4 and N20 = 296. LCA was conducted using SimaPro software and mainly literature data, supplemented with expert-estimates when needed. Detailed information on the used data is presented in the following sections.
8: The climate impacts of the anaerobic digestion chain was ana- lysed from the entire life cycle for both end products - that is, biogas for energy, and digestate for fertilisers - using the functional units MJenergy and kgnitrogen- Accordingly, the results are presented as g CO2 ekv. per MJ of energy produced and kg CO2 ekv. per kg of nitrogen (N) in digestate. The LCA was conducted in two phases (phase 1 and 2; Fig. 1).

As for the simplified chain description (Fig. 1), phase 1 starts from pig slurry storage with floating cover at farm following by co- feedstock procurement (slurry mixing and separation of slurry for solid fraction, grass mowing from uncultivated lands or storage of side streams by the food industry at its place of origin). The emis- sions from generation phases of slurry and co-feedstocks are not included in the assessment work, since they are considered as waste materials according to EU (2009) and these emissions are allocated for the main products e.g. piggery electricity consumption is allocated to the main product of pork meat. However, the project has taken into account emissions from slurry storage, as the biogas process reduces emissions by shorter storage times (normally it is stored in the farm year-round but in the biogas chains it is trans- ported at regular intervals to the biogas plant). After storage, the slurry is mixed and pumped to a semitrailer truck for trans- portation to the biogas plant. Also after the co-feedstock pro- curements the co-feedstock is pumped to a semitrailer truck for transportation to the biogas plant.

Both slurry and co-feedstock are placed in pre-storage at the biogas plant before pumped to the reactor (side streams from food industry are also went through hygienisation). After digestion (through the biogas process), the biogas is delivered to the CHP unit producing electricity and digestate is stored for a while in covered post-digestion tank and after that separated to solid and liquid fractions. The electricity consumption of separation, hygienisation etc. is deducted from energy plant's own production. Phase 2 in- cludes the pumping of separated liquid and solid fractions of digestate to the transportation back to farms. The fractions are stored covered at the farm, and after the storage are pumped for transportation and spread on the field.

More specifically, this study compared three different theoret- ical anaerobic digestion chains as scenarios 1-3 (S1-S3), which consisted of different feedstock mixtures and their procurement (Table 1). Scenarios were defined in cooperation with Finnish biogas plant operators to correspond to realistic and applicable practices (Figs. 2-4). In all the scenarios, the chosen feedstock processing capacity was 19,500 which is a typical capacity for Finnish biogas plants processing manure from several animal farms. The main feedstock in all the scenarios was 16,000 tonnes of pig slurry transported to biogas plant every two weeks with semi- trailer trucks (leaving the plant at the same time with digestate to the same farms, thus avoiding empty loads). At the biogas plant, the
Fig. 1. The simplified anaerobic digestion chain evaluated in this study.
Table 1 The feedstocks used in the assessed anaerobic digestion chains in the different scenarios.

Scenarios|Feedstock|Amount, t/a S1|Pig slurry|16,000 |Solid fraction of separated pig slurry|3500 (from 20,792 of slurry) S2|Pig slurry|16,000 |Grass from uncultivated fields|3500 S3|Pig slurry|16,000 |Side streams from the food industry|3500

slurry is received in a covered reception tank to avoid emissions before feeding to the reactor. The scenarios were differentiated with co-feedstocks (3500 t/a) and their procurements before feeding to the reactor in biogas plant. The co-feedstock in S1 and S2 were chosen due they are seen to increase biogas production of the plant compared to mere slurry as feedstock. The co-feedstock in S3 is seen as a source of gate fees, which are often considered desirable for economic reasons (Luostarinen et al., 2011; Rasi et al., 2012).

In S1 (Fig. 2 the assessment begins with short-term storage of pig slurry (36,792 t per year) on farm. After mixing and separation of slurry, 16,000 tonnes of it is pumped and transported with semi- trailer truck and used as the main feedstock in the biogas plant. The rest of the slurry (20,792 is mechanically separated at the farms to produce solid and liquid fractions. The 3500 tonnes of solid fraction was transported to the biogas plant as a co-feedstock to pig slurry. The liquid fraction was not seen as part of biogas production and was therefore excluded from the calculation. At the biogas plant, main feedstock and co-feedstocks are received in a covered reception tank to avoid emissions before feeding to the reactor. After digestion, the biogas is delivered to the CHP unit producing electricity (1838 MWh/a) and heat (2625 MWh/a) as well as digestate (18,436t), which is stored for a while in a covered post- digestion tank and then separated into solid (3103 t) and liquid (15,333 fractions. The assessment of S2 (Fig. 3) begins with short-term pig slurry (16,000t) storage on the farm and harvesting of the grass from uncultivated fields (3500t) as co-feedstock. Once a year the co- feedstock is transported to the biogas plant and stored in covered silos. After digestion the biogas is delivered to the CHP unit pro- ducing electricity (2133 MWh/a) and heat (3048 MWh/a) as well as digestate (18,265t) which is stored for a while in covered post-
digestion tank and then separated into solid (3075 and liquid (15,190 fractions.

S3 (Fig. 4) starts from short-term pig slurry (16,000 storage on the farm and storage of the food industry side streams at the food industry plant in covered tanks. After the transportation of the co- feedstock to the biogas plant, the vehicle returned with empty cargo to the food industry and the co-feedstock at the plant were collected into a covered tank and hygienised (1 h, 70 °C) prior to initiation of the biogas process. After digestion the biogas is delivered to the CHP unit producing electricity (1597 MWh/a) and heat (2281 MWh/a) as well as digestate (18,575t) which is stored for a while in a covered post-digestion tank and then separated into solid (3127 and liquid (15,448 t) fractions.

In all three scenarios, the biogas that is produced is converted to heat and electricity in the CHP unit. Part of the produced energy is used at the biogas plant, e.g. for reactor heating and mixing. The energy share available for sale was only assessed as an energy quantity. Losses during energy transmission from plant were not included in the calculations.

The post-digestion tank acts as digestate storage prior to its mechanical separation into liquid and solid fractions. The energy consumption of separation was considered as part of the CHP production using the produced biogas. After the separation the solid and liquid fractions are transported back to the farms that produced the original slurry. The two fractions are stored at the farms in covered tanks for a maximum of 10-11 months and transported to the fields by tractors. From the field, the tractor returns with empty cargo. Direct and indirect N20 emissions from spreading of the separated digestate fractions on the fields was calculated according to IPCC 2006.
Fig. 2. System boundary of S1, biogas processing of pig slurry (16,000 t/. and solid fraction of separated pig slurry (3500 t/a) as feedstocks.
9: The climate impacts of the anaerobic digestion chain was ana- lysed from the entire life cycle for both end products - that is, biogas for energy, and digestate for fertilisers - using the functional units MJenergy and kgnitrogen- Accordingly, the results are presented as g CO2 ekv. per MJ of energy produced and kg CO2 ekv. per kg of nitrogen (N) in digestate. The LCA was conducted in two phases (phase 1 and 2; Fig. 1).

As for the simplified chain description (Fig. 1), phase 1 starts from pig slurry storage with floating cover at farm following by co- feedstock procurement (slurry mixing and separation of slurry for solid fraction, grass mowing from uncultivated lands or storage of side streams by the food industry at its place of origin). The emis- sions from generation phases of slurry and co-feedstocks are not included in the assessment work, since they are considered as waste materials according to EU (2009) and these emissions are allocated for the main products e.g. piggery electricity consumption is allocated to the main product of pork meat. However, the project has taken into account emissions from slurry storage, as the biogas process reduces emissions by shorter storage times (normally it is stored in the farm year-round but in the biogas chains it is trans- ported at regular intervals to the biogas plant). After storage, the slurry is mixed and pumped to a semitrailer truck for trans- portation to the biogas plant. Also after the co-feedstock pro- curements the co-feedstock is pumped to a semitrailer truck for transportation to the biogas plant.

Both slurry and co-feedstock are placed in pre-storage at the biogas plant before pumped to the reactor (side streams from food industry are also went through hygienisation). After digestion (through the biogas process), the biogas is delivered to the CHP unit producing electricity and digestate is stored for a while in covered post-digestion tank and after that separated to solid and liquid fractions. The electricity consumption of separation, hygienisation etc. is deducted from energy plant's own production. Phase 2 in- cludes the pumping of separated liquid and solid fractions of digestate to the transportation back to farms. The fractions are stored covered at the farm, and after the storage are pumped for transportation and spread on the field.

More specifically, this study compared three different theoret- ical anaerobic digestion chains as scenarios 1-3 (S1-S3), which consisted of different feedstock mixtures and their procurement (Table 1). Scenarios were defined in cooperation with Finnish biogas plant operators to correspond to realistic and applicable practices (Figs. 2-4). In all the scenarios, the chosen feedstock processing capacity was 19,500 which is a typical capacity for Finnish biogas plants processing manure from several animal farms. The main feedstock in all the scenarios was 16,000 tonnes of pig slurry transported to biogas plant every two weeks with semi- trailer trucks (leaving the plant at the same time with digestate to the same farms, thus avoiding empty loads). The two fractions are stored at the farms in covered tanks for a maximum of 10-11 months and transported to the fields by tractors. From the field, the tractor returns with empty cargo. Direct and indirect N20 emissions from spreading of the separated digestate fractions on the fields was calculated according to IPCC 2006.

13: The climate impacts of the anaerobic digestion chain was ana- lysed from the entire life cycle for both end products - that is, biogas for energy, and digestate for fertilisers - using the functional units MJenergy and kgnitrogen- Accordingly, the results are presented as g CO2 ekv. per MJ of energy produced and kg CO2 ekv. per kg of nitrogen (N) in digestate. The LCA was conducted in two phases (phase 1 and 2; Fig. 1).

As for the simplified chain description (Fig. 1), phase 1 starts from pig slurry storage with floating cover at farm following by co- feedstock procurement (slurry mixing and separation of slurry for solid fraction, grass mowing from uncultivated lands or storage of side streams by the food industry at its place of origin). The emis- sions from generation phases of slurry and co-feedstocks are not included in the assessment work, since they are considered as waste materials according to EU (2009) and these emissions are allocated for the main products e.g. piggery electricity consumption is allocated to the main product of pork meat. However, the project has taken into account emissions from slurry storage, as the biogas process reduces emissions by shorter storage times (normally it is stored in the farm year-round but in the biogas chains it is trans- ported at regular intervals to the biogas plant). After storage, the slurry is mixed and pumped to a semitrailer truck for trans- portation to the biogas plant. Also after the co-feedstock pro- curements the co-feedstock is pumped to a semitrailer truck for transportation to the biogas plant.

Both slurry and co-feedstock are placed in pre-storage at the biogas plant before pumped to the reactor (side streams from food industry are also went through hygienisation). After digestion (through the biogas process), the biogas is delivered to the CHP unit producing electricity and digestate is stored for a while in covered post-digestion tank and after that separated to solid and liquid fractions. The electricity consumption of separation, hygienisation etc. is deducted from energy plant's own production. Phase 2 in- cludes the pumping of separated liquid and solid fractions of digestate to the transportation back to farms. The fractions are stored covered at the farm, and after the storage are pumped for transportation and spread on the field.

More specifically, this study compared three different theoret- ical anaerobic digestion chains as scenarios 1-3 (S1-S3), which consisted of different feedstock mixtures and their procurement (Table 1). Scenarios were defined in cooperation with Finnish biogas plant operators to correspond to realistic and applicable practices (Figs. 2-4). In all the scenarios, the chosen feedstock processing capacity was 19,500 which is a typical capacity for Finnish biogas plants processing manure from several animal farms. The main feedstock in all the scenarios was 16,000 tonnes of pig slurry transported to biogas plant every two weeks with semi- trailer trucks (leaving the plant at the same time with digestate to the same farms, thus avoiding empty loads). At the biogas plant, the
Fig. 1. The simplified anaerobic digestion chain evaluated in this study.
Table 1 The feedstocks used in the assessed anaerobic digestion chains in the different scenarios.

Scenarios|Feedstock|Amount, t/a S1|Pig slurry|16,000 |Solid fraction of separated pig slurry|3500 (from 20,792 of slurry) S2|Pig slurry|16,000 |Grass from uncultivated fields|3500 S3|Pig slurry|16,000 |Side streams from the food industry|3500

slurry is received in a covered reception tank to avoid emissions before feeding to the reactor. The scenarios were differentiated with co-feedstocks (3500 t/a) and their procurements before feeding to the reactor in biogas plant. The co-feedstock in S1 and S2 were chosen due they are seen to increase biogas production of the plant compared to mere slurry as feedstock. The co-feedstock in S3 is seen as a source of gate fees, which are often considered desirable for economic reasons (Luostarinen et al., 2011; Rasi et al., 2012).

In S1 (Fig. 2 the assessment begins with short-term storage of pig slurry (36,792 t per year) on farm. After mixing and separation of slurry, 16,000 tonnes of it is pumped and transported with semi- trailer truck and used as the main feedstock in the biogas plant. The rest of the slurry (20,792 is mechanically separated at the farms to produce solid and liquid fractions. The 3500 tonnes of solid fraction was transported to the biogas plant as a co-feedstock to pig slurry. The liquid fraction was not seen as part of biogas production and was therefore excluded from the calculation. At the biogas plant, main feedstock and co-feedstocks are received in a covered reception tank to avoid emissions before feeding to the reactor. After digestion, the biogas is delivered to the CHP unit producing electricity (1838 MWh/a) and heat (2625 MWh/a) as well as digestate (18,436t), which is stored for a while in a covered post- digestion tank and then separated into solid (3103 t) and liquid (15,333 fractions. The assessment of S2 (Fig. 3) begins with short-term pig slurry (16,000t) storage on the farm and harvesting of the grass from uncultivated fields (3500t) as co-feedstock. Once a year the co- feedstock is transported to the biogas plant and stored in covered silos. After digestion the biogas is delivered to the CHP unit pro- ducing electricity (2133 MWh/a) and heat (3048 MWh/a) as well as digestate (18,265t) which is stored for a while in covered post-
digestion tank and then separated into solid (3075 and liquid (15,190 fractions.

S3 (Fig. 4) starts from short-term pig slurry (16,000 storage on the farm and storage of the food industry side streams at the food industry plant in covered tanks. After the transportation of the co- feedstock to the biogas plant, the vehicle returned with empty cargo to the food industry and the co-feedstock at the plant were collected into a covered tank and hygienised (1 h, 70 °C) prior to initiation of the biogas process. After digestion the biogas is delivered to the CHP unit producing electricity (1597 MWh/a) and heat (2281 MWh/a) as well as digestate (18,575t) which is stored for a while in a covered post-digestion tank and then separated into solid (3127 and liquid (15,448 t) fractions.

In all three scenarios, the biogas that is produced is converted to heat and electricity in the CHP unit. Part of the produced energy is used at the biogas plant, e.g. for reactor heating and mixing. The energy share available for sale was only assessed as an energy quantity. Losses during energy transmission from plant were not included in the calculations.

The post-digestion tank acts as digestate storage prior to its mechanical separation into liquid and solid fractions. The energy consumption of separation was considered as part of the CHP production using the produced biogas. After the separation the solid and liquid fractions are transported back to the farms that produced the original slurry. The two fractions are stored at the farms in covered tanks for a maximum of 10-11 months and transported to the fields by tractors. From the field, the tractor returns with empty cargo. Direct and indirect N20 emissions from spreading of the separated digestate fractions on the fields was calculated according to IPCC 2006.

17: The transportation distances from the pig farms to the biogas plant and back were based on typical practise on Finnish biogas plants (Table 2). The separated solid fractions of slurry and of digestate could be transported over longer distances due to their lower water content as compared to slurry and liquid fraction. The emissions of the transportation are based on LIPASTO database, in which the calculations are based on tonnes per kilometre and not on fuel consumption (see section 2.5.). Estimates of fuel consumption required for the transportation, spreading, pumping and mixing of slurry, separated slurry fractions and the separated fractions from digestate (tractor, diesel) are presented in Table 3. The amount of slurry and digestate per application area on the field was estimated based on the amount of phosphorus in the raw materials, assuming they are used for barley cultivation in clay soil. The average P-value of clay soil is 8.03 (Eurofins Viljavuuspalvelu Oy, 2014), whereby the permitted phosphorus application rate is 14 kg/ha (Nummela and Tuononen, 2009).
18: The characteristics of the feedstocks were evaluated in the basis of the average literature values (Table 4). The composition of the separated solid fraction from pig slurry (S1) was calculated with mass balances using the characteristics of pig slurry and the following separation efficiency (in solid fraction) of a decanter

centrifuge: mass 17%, total solids (TS) 67%, volatile solids (VS) 67%, nitrogen (N) 30%, ammonium nitrogen (NH4-N) 19%, phosphorus (P) 74% and potassium (K) 12% (Hjorth et al., 2010). The same centrifuge, with similar separation efficiency, was assumed to be used for separating the digestate also. For food industry side streams (S3), an average composition of different side streams was used (see Table 5).
19: <header>2.4. Calculation of energy and mass balance </header>

The energy production of the biogas plant was calculated based on the biochemical methane potential (BMP) and volatile solids (VS) content of the feedstocks (Table 4), and the energy production efficiencies in the CHP (35% for electricity, 50% for heat; Murphy et al., 2004). The calculation of energy and mass balances for the anaerobic digestion plants in each scenario was based on literature values. The energy consumed at the biogas plant was considered for the following singular processes: biogas process, digestate sepa- ration and hygienisation (latter in S3 only in addition to other processes). However, slurry separation on farms (only in S1) was not considered to consume energy produced by CHP plant but consumed electricity (average Finnish) and these emissions were assessed separately from CHP plant and as part of feedstock handling on farms.

The electricity consumption of the centrifuge separating both slurry and digestate was assumed at 2.5 kWh/t of processed ma- terial (Moller et al., 2009). Electricity required for mixing in conjunction to hygienisation was assumed to be 0.085 kWh/t of

processed material (based on discussions with Finnish biogas plant operators) and the electricity consumption of the biogas plant was 3% of the biogas produced (Pöschl et al., 2010). Estimation of the heat requirement of the biogas process took account of heating of the feedstock and the heat losses within the reactor. The amount of energy needed for feedstock heating was calculated using the specific heat capacity of water (4.18 k kj/kg°C), feedstock mass and the temperature change from 15 to 40 °C (Tampio et al., 2016). In scenario S3, the hygienisation of the food industry side streams was calculated similarly, but using a temperature change from 15 to 75 °C. Heat losses were calculated as annual averages using the reactor volume of 1290 m³, u-value of 0.53 W/m2, reactor tem- perature of 37 and monthly average outside temperature in Southern Finland (data from the Finnish Meteorological Institute). Mass balance of the digestion process was based on feedstock characteristics, biogas composition (60% CH4 and 40% CO2) and component densities (CH4 0.72 kg/m3 3 CO2 1.96 kg/m as in Tampio et al., 2016). Due to the increased solubilisation of nitrogen during digestion, the amount ammonium nitrogen (t/a) was assumed to increase 50% in grass and food industry side streams (Jagadabhi et al., 2011) and slightly less, 30%, in the pig slurry and separated solid fraction of pig slurry.

2: N20 and methane emissions of pig slurry, solid and liquid fraction storage were based on the national GHG inventory (Statistics Finland, 2013). Emissions of CHP in the biogas plant were based on Danish measurements (Kristensen et al., 2004). The emissions of transporting and applying manure and digested solid

and liquid fractions were calculated based on fuel consumption (Grönroos and Voutilainen, 2001). The direct and indirect N20 emissions for field use of digestate-separated fractions were calculated by IPCC (2006) coefficients.

The emission factors for fossil diesel used in working machines and for transport are based on the LIPASTO database of VTT Technical Research Centre of Finland Ltd (2008a, b). The emis- sions for mineral fertiliser production were calculated based on DNV GL (2015) and emissions for transport and field use of mineral fertilisers were calculated by IPCC (2006) coefficients, taking into account direct and indirect N20 emissions.
3: Allocation work was carried out for emissions occurring in phase 1 and for the two end products, namely biogas as energy and digestate as fertiliser. First, in this study all annual emissions were either allocated only for the energy according to the EU Renewable Energy Directive (EU, 2009) on the basis of the lower calorific values of the products which means almost all emissions are allo- cated to biogas due the calorific value of the digestate is very low. Second, the allocation was made by the economic allocation method, which is based on the estimated economic values of the energy and the nutrients taken from Finnish Biogas Calculator (www.biokaasulaskuri.fi), which uses economic values of mineral fertilisers from year 2014 as a base for nutrients (Table According to the Biogas Calculator, 29-32% of emissions were allocated to energy and 64-71% to nutrients, depending on the differences in energy production and digestate nutrient amounts per feedstock scenario (Table 7). 9: The LCA of three anaerobic digestion scenarios of utilizing pig slurry and different co-feedstock (S1-S3) were conducted. The emissions were allocated either to only energy or both energy and digestate using mass and economic allocation method and compared to production of fossil energy and mineral fertilisers. In addition, these emissions allocated for digestate were further combined with the emissions of digestate storage, transportation and utilization as fertilizers on field and compared to utilization of mineral fertilizers on field.
3.1. Climate emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for energy and digestate

When emissions from phase 1 are allocated only to the energy, they varied between 14.9 and 19.6g CO2eq per MJ, depending on the scenario (S1-S3) (Fig. 5). When using economic allocation, the emissions allocated for energy were reduced (partly allocated to digestate) and ranged from 4.7 to 7.2 CO2eq per MJ between scenarios S1-S3. In case of mass allocation, emissions for energy decreased even below 1 g CO2eq per MJ with all scenarios, because the mass of the digestate is much higher compared to the mass of the biogas. The emissions allocated for energy was highest with scenario using grass from uncultivated fields as co-feedstock (S2) and lowest with scenario using side streams from the food industry (S3). The energy content of scenario 2 feedstocks was highest
10: The LCA of three anaerobic digestion scenarios of utilizing pig slurry and different co-feedstock (S1-S3) were conducted. The emissions were allocated either to only energy or both energy and digestate using mass and economic allocation method and compared to production of fossil energy and mineral fertilisers. In addition, these emissions allocated for digestate were further combined with the emissions of digestate storage, transportation and utilization as fertilizers on field and compared to utilization of mineral fertilizers on field.
3.1. Climate emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for energy and digestate

When emissions from phase 1 are allocated only to the energy, they varied between 14.9 and 19.6g CO2eq per MJ, depending on the scenario (S1-S3) (Fig. 5). When using economic allocation, the emissions allocated for energy were reduced (partly allocated to digestate) and ranged from 4.7 to 7.2 CO2eq per MJ between scenarios S1-S3. In case of mass allocation, emissions for energy decreased even below 1 g CO2eq per MJ with all scenarios, because the mass of the digestate is much higher compared to the mass of the biogas. The emissions allocated for energy was highest with scenario using grass from uncultivated fields as co-feedstock (S2) and lowest with scenario using side streams from the food industry (S3). The energy content of scenario 2 feedstocks was highest

leading to highest CHP plant emissions and lowest with scenario 3. In addition, the N20 emissions from grasslands (S2) are higher than the climate emissions from additional slurry storage (S1) and covered storage (S3). These results are in line with Hijazi et al. (2016) review where it was observed that the composition of the feedstocks may affect the climate impacts for energy as well as the energy emissions decrease when moving towards societal and physical allocation methods towards economic and mass allocation methods.

Compared to an equivalent fossil energy option of natural gas production (almost 70g CO2eq per MJ, Ecoinvent), the results showed that emissions for anaerobic digestion allocated for energy has a significantly lower emissions in all three scenarios despite the allocation method used (Fig. 5). These results are in line with study results by Paolini et al., (2018), Hijazi et al., 2016, Winquist et al., (2015) and Rasi et al., (2012).

The largest share of climate emissions for energy (Fig. 6) in all scenarios with all allocation methods was generated by CHP plant including both emissions from producing (pre-storage, digestate separation, hygienisation, pumping of feedstocks in reactor and production of energy). However, in case of CHP plant, it is utilizing the self-produced energy so the emissions bought elsewhere are avoided and the total amount of emissions during anaerobic digestion process are targeted for the end-product energy (MJ) to be sold.

The second largest share of climate emissions for energy was produced during feedstock handling (slurry storage and co- feedstock procurement) with greater variation between scenarios due differences in co-feedstock handling practices (mixing and separation of slurry for solid fraction in S1, grass mowing from uncultivated lands in S2 and storage of side streams by the food industry at its place of origin in S1): N20 emissions from grasslands (S2) were the most significant, followed by slurry storage with floating cover and procurements (S1) and led to lowest emissions due covered storage in food industry and zero emissions during production (S3).

The transportation of feedstock (including pumping of feed- stocks to vehicle) had the lowest share of emissions during anaer- obic digestion (phase 1). Emissions from empty cargo was avoided when the semi-trailer truck transporting the slurry and grass to the biogas plant (included phase 1) was assumed to have returned to
Fig. 5. Climate emissions of biogas-based energy produced from different feedstocks using different allocation methods in comparison to using fossil energy.

Fig. 6. Climate emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for energy during feedstock handling, transport and CHP plant (phase 1) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and economic and mass allocation methods.
the same farms with the digested fractions (avoided in phase 1 and included in phase 2).

However, this is comparison between anaerobic digestion pro- cesses with significantly lower emissions compared to fossil en- ergy. A longer time of slurry storage in farms would emerge without digestion option: the slurry transportation every two weeks from the farm to the biogas plant decreased the farm storage time by 80%. The significantly smallest share of emissions was with transportation of feedstock.

The emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate (Fig. 7) are naturally zero when 100% of production is allocated to energy, but with economic allocation emissions the emissions levels varied between 1.1 and 1.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on the scenario (S1-S3). With mass allocation emissions higher, between 1.5 and 2.4kg CO2 eq/kg N. These results are in line with Hijazi et al. (2016) review observations where digestate emissions were seen to increase when moving towards societal and physical allocation methods towards economic and mass allocation methods. The scenarios with highest and lowest emissions for digestate was the
same as for energy: highest with scenario 2 (S2) and lowest with scenario 3 (S3) (see Fig. 8).

The results showed that with each of the allocation methods and scenarios the emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate has lower climate emissions than mineral fertiliser pro- duction (5.3 kg CO2eq/kg N, DNV GL, 2015), which is in line with the estimate made by Winquist et al. (2015).

When considering the emissions for anaerobic digestion allo- cated for digestate (phase 1), the largest share of climate emissions was generated during CHP processing in case with economic and mass allocation methods and with scenarios 1 and 3. In scenario 2, CHP emissions were the same as with feedstock handling (pro- curement, storage, mixing, separation) with both economic and mass allocation methods. This is maybe because S2 feedstock contains highest amount of N and the emissions in case with digestate are allocated per N and not per MJ as with energy emis- sions. The transportation of feedstock had lowest share of emis- sions with all scenarios.
Fig. 7. Climate emissions of biogas-based digestate produced from different feedstocks using different allocation methods in comparison to mineral fertiliser production.

Fig. 8. Climate emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate during feedstock handling, transport and CHP plant (phase 1) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and economic and mass allocation methods.
3.2. Climate emissions of digestate utilization as fertiliser on field

During storage, transportation and use of digestate, the climate emissions are between 8.2 and 8.4kg CO2 eq/kg N in scenarios S1-S3. These emissions are assessed only for digestate and are not therefore effected by allocation methods and depending only on different co-feedstocks, nutrient contents and masses in scenarios S1-S3 (phase 2, Fig. 9).

The climate emissions in all scenarios S1-S3 - are higher than those of mineral fertiliser storage, transportation and use (6.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N, calculated with IPCC coefficients). This is because mineral nitrogen fertilisers do not generate emissions during storage but the digested fractions may lose nitrogen as evaporated ammonia (0.5 and 0.6kg CO2 eq/kg N) during storage. In addition,

transportation of the digestate from biogas plant to the storage and to the field cause more emissions (0.7kg CO2eq/kg N in all sce- narios) than the transportation of mineral fertilisers from industry to the farm and to the field (0.09 kg CO2 eq/kg N, calculated with IPCC coefficients), although the distance to the industry to the farm was much longer for the mineral fertilisers (200 km) than the distance from plant to the storage at farm (20 km) for the digestate. This is because the emissions decrease with increasing nutrient content of the material and the digestate has less nutrients and requires more transportation to the field to achieve the same fer- tilising effect as more concentrated mineral fertilisers. Nitrogen content of mineral fertilisers as a reference can vary considerably and therefore in this study the mineral fertilisation was assumed to be conducted with similar nutrient amount per hectare as in the

digested fractions and nitrogen content was assumed to average of those used in Finland. In addition, the direct N20 emissions from organic fertilisers (4.7 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) are little bit higher than with mineral fertilizers (4.6 kg CO2eq/kg N). Also the indirect N20 emissions are higher with digestate (2.0 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) than with mineral fertilisers (1.5 kg CO2eq/kg N). The digested fractions could also be processed further to receive more concentrated products resembling mineral fertilisers (Tampio et al., 2016; Vaneechaute et al., 2017). This might be viable in larger, regional biogas plants, reducing emissions from manure fertiliser use and replacing mineral fertilisers. In Finland, it has been esti- mated that manure phosphorus could cover all phosphorus fertil- isation required by cereals and grass if the manure could partly be transported from intensive animal production areas to regions needing the nutrients (Ylivainio et al., 2014). Also farm-scale biogas plants may improve the farm's nutrient self-sufficiency via improving the solubility of nitrogen (Winquist et al., 2015) and recycling the farm's excess plant biomasses, such as the grass from uncultivated fields in this study.

The highest climate emission for digestate storage, trans- portation and field use (phase 2) was with scenario 1 using sepa- rated solid fraction of pig slurry as co-feedstock (8.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N). The emissions were little bit lower and the same with scenario 2 and scenario 3 (8.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N).

The total emissions (Fig. 10) for digestate are received by combining the allocated emissions of anaerobic digestion for digestate (phase 1) mentioned in previous chapter (3.1.), with storage, transportation and use (phase 2). When emission are allocated 100% for energy during phase 1 it is leading total emis- sions of digestate to be the same as viewed during phase 2 (be- tween 8.2 and 8.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N). However, with economic allocation total emissions for digestate rises between 9.4 and 9.9 kg CO2 eq/kg N, and with mass allocation method even more between 9.9 and 10.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on different co-feedstocks in scenarios (S1-S3). This is because the production emissions of mineral fertilisers are significantly higher than emissons from anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate, they compensating the higher emissions of digestate during storage, transportation and field use. Therefore, the total emissions of digestate with all
allocation methods and in all scenarios were lower than emissions for mineral fertiliser production, transportation and use (11.71 kg CO2 eq/kg N).

Comparing the scenarios with different co-feedstocks, the highest total emissions for digestate was with S1 (8.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N) and lowest with S2 (8.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N) when during anaerobic digestion (phase 1) 100% of emissions were allocated to energy. In case of economic allocation the highest total emissions were again with S1 (10.0 kg CO2 eq/kg N) but now the lowest turned out to be with S3 (9.3 kg CO2 eq/kg N) due smallest emission impact during anaerobic digestion (phase 1). In case of mass allocation the highest emissions were the same with scenario S1 and S2 (10.6 kg CO2 eq/ kg N) and lowest with S3 (9.7 kg CO2 eq/kg N) (Fig. 10).

The highest climate emissions came from fertiliser use of the digested fractions on the field (Fig. 11) ranging between 6.9 and 7.1 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending slightly on scenarios (S1-S3). The field use as one of the highest source for emissions has been perceived also by Rehl and Müller (2011), Rasi et al. (2012), Brockmann et al. (2014) and Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015). The dominant sources for emissions are direct N20 emissions (4.7 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) and the indirect N20 emissions (2.0 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) from organic fertiliser application on the field.

The next following emission sources did not show any more significant differences. The climate emissions of transportation during phase 2 was the same (0.7 kg CO2 eq/kg N) with all sce- narios (S1-S3) and emissions during digestate storage were a bit lower between 0.5 and 0.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending slightly on scenarios (S1-S3). In this study, covered storages were presently assumed to minimise the emissions from digestate use (Edelmann et al., 2001; Boulamanti et al., 2013; Mezzullo et al., 2013; Bachmaier et al., 2013) and open storage of digested residues is usually considered one of the biggest sources of emissions (Hijazi et al., 2016). In addition, the emissions during feedstock handling were between 0.0 and 1.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N and during CHP pro- cessing between 0.0 and 1.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on sce- narios (S1-S3) and allocation method. When we combined the emissions of transportation during phase 1 (0.0 and 0.1 kg CO2 eq/ kg N) with transportation during phase 2 the emissions were
13: Amounts of energy for sale (after own use) and of nutrients produced in the different scenarios, and allocation factors between energy and nutrients.

leading to highest CHP plant emissions and lowest with scenario 3. In addition, the N20 emissions from grasslands (S2) are higher than the climate emissions from additional slurry storage (S1) and covered storage (S3). These results are in line with Hijazi et al. (2016) review where it was observed that the composition of the feedstocks may affect the climate impacts for energy as well as the energy emissions decrease when moving towards societal and physical allocation methods towards economic and mass allocation methods.

Compared to an equivalent fossil energy option of natural gas production (almost 70g CO2eq per MJ, Ecoinvent), the results showed that emissions for anaerobic digestion allocated for energy has a significantly lower emissions in all three scenarios despite the allocation method used (Fig. 5). These results are in line with study results by Paolini et al., (2018), Hijazi et al., 2016, Winquist et al., (2015) and Rasi et al., (2012).

The largest share of climate emissions for energy (Fig. 6) in all scenarios with all allocation methods was generated by CHP plant including both emissions from producing (pre-storage, digestate separation, hygienisation, pumping of feedstocks in reactor and production of energy). However, in case of CHP plant, it is utilizing the self-produced energy so the emissions bought elsewhere are avoided and the total amount of emissions during anaerobic digestion process are targeted for the end-product energy (MJ) to be sold.

The second largest share of climate emissions for energy was produced during feedstock handling (slurry storage and co- feedstock procurement) with greater variation between scenarios due differences in co-feedstock handling practices (mixing and separation of slurry for solid fraction in S1, grass mowing from uncultivated lands in S2 and storage of side streams by the food industry at its place of origin in S1): N20 emissions from grasslands (S2) were the most significant, followed by slurry storage with floating cover and procurements (S1) and led to lowest emissions due covered storage in food industry and zero emissions during production (S3).

The transportation of feedstock (including pumping of feed- stocks to vehicle) had the lowest share of emissions during anaer- obic digestion (phase 1). Emissions from empty cargo was avoided when the semi-trailer truck transporting the slurry and grass to the biogas plant (included phase 1) was assumed to have returned to
Fig. 5. Climate emissions of biogas-based energy produced from different feedstocks using different allocation methods in comparison to using fossil energy.

Fig. 6. Climate emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for energy during feedstock handling, transport and CHP plant (phase 1) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and economic and mass allocation methods.
the same farms with the digested fractions (avoided in phase 1 and included in phase 2).

However, this is comparison between anaerobic digestion pro- cesses with significantly lower emissions compared to fossil en- ergy. A longer time of slurry storage in farms would emerge without digestion option: the slurry transportation every two weeks from the farm to the biogas plant decreased the farm storage time by 80%. The significantly smallest share of emissions was with transportation of feedstock.

The emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate (Fig. 7) are naturally zero when 100% of production is allocated to energy, but with economic allocation emissions the emissions levels varied between 1.1 and 1.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on the scenario (S1-S3). With mass allocation emissions higher, between 1.5 and 2.4kg CO2 eq/kg N. These results are in line with Hijazi et al. (2016) review observations where digestate emissions were seen to increase when moving towards societal and physical allocation methods towards economic and mass allocation methods. The scenarios with highest and lowest emissions for digestate was the
same as for energy: highest with scenario 2 (S2) and lowest with scenario 3 (S3) (see Fig. 8).

The results showed that with each of the allocation methods and scenarios the emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate has lower climate emissions than mineral fertiliser pro- duction (5.3 kg CO2eq/kg N, DNV GL, 2015), which is in line with the estimate made by Winquist et al. (2015).

When considering the emissions for anaerobic digestion allo- cated for digestate (phase 1), the largest share of climate emissions was generated during CHP processing in case with economic and mass allocation methods and with scenarios 1 and 3. In scenario 2, CHP emissions were the same as with feedstock handling (pro- curement, storage, mixing, separation) with both economic and mass allocation methods. This is maybe because S2 feedstock contains highest amount of N and the emissions in case with digestate are allocated per N and not per MJ as with energy emis- sions. The transportation of feedstock had lowest share of emis- sions with all scenarios.

Fig. 8. Climate emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate during feedstock handling, transport and CHP plant (phase 1) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and economic and mass allocation methods.
3.2. Climate emissions of digestate utilization as fertiliser on field

During storage, transportation and use of digestate, the climate emissions are between 8.2 and 8.4kg CO2 eq/kg N in scenarios S1-S3. These emissions are assessed only for digestate and are not therefore effected by allocation methods and depending only on different co-feedstocks, nutrient contents and masses in scenarios S1-S3 (phase 2, Fig. 9).

The climate emissions in all scenarios S1-S3 - are higher than those of mineral fertiliser storage, transportation and use (6.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N, calculated with IPCC coefficients). This is because mineral nitrogen fertilisers do not generate emissions during storage but the digested fractions may lose nitrogen as evaporated ammonia (0.5 and 0.6kg CO2 eq/kg N) during storage. In addition,

transportation of the digestate from biogas plant to the storage and to the field cause more emissions (0.7kg CO2eq/kg N in all sce- narios) than the transportation of mineral fertilisers from industry to the farm and to the field (0.09 kg CO2 eq/kg N, calculated with IPCC coefficients), although the distance to the industry to the farm was much longer for the mineral fertilisers (200 km) than the distance from plant to the storage at farm (20 km) for the digestate. This is because the emissions decrease with increasing nutrient content of the material and the digestate has less nutrients and requires more transportation to the field to achieve the same fer- tilising effect as more concentrated mineral fertilisers. Nitrogen content of mineral fertilisers as a reference can vary considerably and therefore in this study the mineral fertilisation was assumed to be conducted with similar nutrient amount per hectare as in the

12||||| "10 8 6 4 2 0"||||| "10 8 6 4 2 0"|||||Transportation and use of mineral fertiliser "10 8 6 4 2 0"|||||S1: Phase 2 S2: Phase 2 S3: Phase 2 ||||| ||||| ||100% allocation to energy|Economic allocation|Mass allocation|

Fig. 9. Climate emissions of mineral fertiliser storage, transportation and usage compared to climate emissions of digestate storage, transportation and use (Phase 2) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and allocation methods.

digested fractions and nitrogen content was assumed to average of those used in Finland. In addition, the direct N20 emissions from organic fertilisers (4.7 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) are little bit higher than with mineral fertilizers (4.6 kg CO2eq/kg N). Also the indirect N20 emissions are higher with digestate (2.0 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) than with mineral fertilisers (1.5 kg CO2eq/kg N). The digested fractions could also be processed further to receive more concentrated products resembling mineral fertilisers (Tampio et al., 2016; Vaneechaute et al., 2017). This might be viable in larger, regional biogas plants, reducing emissions from manure fertiliser use and replacing mineral fertilisers. In Finland, it has been esti- mated that manure phosphorus could cover all phosphorus fertil- isation required by cereals and grass if the manure could partly be transported from intensive animal production areas to regions needing the nutrients (Ylivainio et al., 2014). Also farm-scale biogas plants may improve the farm's nutrient self-sufficiency via improving the solubility of nitrogen (Winquist et al., 2015) and recycling the farm's excess plant biomasses, such as the grass from uncultivated fields in this study.

The highest climate emission for digestate storage, trans- portation and field use (phase 2) was with scenario 1 using sepa- rated solid fraction of pig slurry as co-feedstock (8.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N). The emissions were little bit lower and the same with scenario 2 and scenario 3 (8.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N).

The total emissions (Fig. 10) for digestate are received by combining the allocated emissions of anaerobic digestion for digestate (phase 1) mentioned in previous chapter (3.1.), with storage, transportation and use (phase 2). When emission are allocated 100% for energy during phase 1 it is leading total emis- sions of digestate to be the same as viewed during phase 2 (be- tween 8.2 and 8.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N). However, with economic allocation total emissions for digestate rises between 9.4 and 9.9 kg CO2 eq/kg N, and with mass allocation method even more between 9.9 and 10.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on different co-feedstocks in scenarios (S1-S3). This is because the production emissions of mineral fertilisers are significantly higher than emissons from anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate, they compensating the higher emissions of digestate during storage, transportation and field use. Therefore, the total emissions of digestate with all
allocation methods and in all scenarios were lower than emissions for mineral fertiliser production, transportation and use (11.71 kg CO2 eq/kg N).

Comparing the scenarios with different co-feedstocks, the highest total emissions for digestate was with S1 (8.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N) and lowest with S2 (8.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N) when during anaerobic digestion (phase 1) 100% of emissions were allocated to energy. 12||||| "10 8 6 4 2 0"||||| "10 8 6 4 2 0"|||||Transportation and use of mineral fertiliser "10 8 6 4 2 0"|||||S1: Phase 2 S2: Phase 2 S3: Phase 2 ||||| ||||| ||100% allocation to energy|Economic allocation|Mass allocation|

Fig. 9. Climate emissions of mineral fertiliser storage, transportation and usage compared to climate emissions of digestate storage, transportation and use (Phase 2) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and allocation methods.

digested fractions and nitrogen content was assumed to average of those used in Finland. In addition, the direct N20 emissions from organic fertilisers (4.7 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) are little bit higher than with mineral fertilizers (4.6 kg CO2eq/kg N). Also the indirect N20 emissions are higher with digestate (2.0 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) than with mineral fertilisers (1.5 kg CO2eq/kg N). The digested fractions could also be processed further to receive more concentrated products resembling mineral fertilisers (Tampio et al., 2016; Vaneechaute et al., 2017). This might be viable in larger, regional biogas plants, reducing emissions from manure fertiliser use and replacing mineral fertilisers. In Finland, it has been esti- mated that manure phosphorus could cover all phosphorus fertil- isation required by cereals and grass if the manure could partly be transported from intensive animal production areas to regions needing the nutrients (Ylivainio et al., 2014). Also farm-scale biogas plants may improve the farm's nutrient self-sufficiency via improving the solubility of nitrogen (Winquist et al., 2015) and recycling the farm's excess plant biomasses, such as the grass from uncultivated fields in this study.

The highest climate emission for digestate storage, trans- portation and field use (phase 2) was with scenario 1 using sepa- rated solid fraction of pig slurry as co-feedstock (8.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N). The emissions were little bit lower and the same with scenario 2 and scenario 3 (8.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N).

The total emissions (Fig. 10) for digestate are received by combining the allocated emissions of anaerobic digestion for digestate (phase 1) mentioned in previous chapter (3.1.), with storage, transportation and use (phase 2). When emission are allocated 100% for energy during phase 1 it is leading total emis- sions of digestate to be the same as viewed during phase 2 (be- tween 8.2 and 8.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N). However, with economic allocation total emissions for digestate rises between 9.4 and 9.9 kg CO2 eq/kg N, and with mass allocation method even more between 9.9 and 10.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on different co-feedstocks in scenarios (S1-S3). This is because the production emissions of mineral fertilisers are significantly higher than emissons from anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate, they compensating the higher emissions of digestate during storage, transportation and field use. Therefore, the total emissions of digestate with all
allocation methods and in all scenarios were lower than emissions for mineral fertiliser production, transportation and use (11.71 kg CO2 eq/kg N).

Comparing the scenarios with different co-feedstocks, the highest total emissions for digestate was with S1 (8.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N) and lowest with S2 (8.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N) when during anaerobic digestion (phase 1) 100% of emissions were allocated to energy. In case of economic allocation the highest total emissions were again with S1 (10.0 kg CO2 eq/kg N) but now the lowest turned out to be with S3 (9.3 kg CO2 eq/kg N) due smallest emission impact during anaerobic digestion (phase 1). In case of mass allocation the highest emissions were the same with scenario S1 and S2 (10.6 kg CO2 eq/ kg N) and lowest with S3 (9.7 kg CO2 eq/kg N) (Fig. 10).

The highest climate emissions came from fertiliser use of the digested fractions on the field (Fig. 11) ranging between 6.9 and 7.1 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending slightly on scenarios (S1-S3). The field use as one of the highest source for emissions has been perceived also by Rehl and Müller (2011), Rasi et al. (2012), Brockmann et al. (2014) and Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015). The dominant sources for emissions are direct N20 emissions (4.7 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) and the indirect N20 emissions (2.0 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) from organic fertiliser application on the field.

The next following emission sources did not show any more significant differences. The climate emissions of transportation during phase 2 was the same (0.7 kg CO2 eq/kg N) with all sce- narios (S1-S3) and emissions during digestate storage were a bit lower between 0.5 and 0.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending slightly on scenarios (S1-S3). In this study, covered storages were presently assumed to minimise the emissions from digestate use (Edelmann et al., 2001; Boulamanti et al., 2013; Mezzullo et al., 2013; Bachmaier et al., 2013) and open storage of digested residues is usually considered one of the biggest sources of emissions (Hijazi et al., 2016). In addition, the emissions during feedstock handling were between 0.0 and 1.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N and during CHP pro- cessing between 0.0 and 1.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on sce- narios (S1-S3) and allocation method. When we combined the emissions of transportation during phase 1 (0.0 and 0.1 kg CO2 eq/ kg N) with transportation during phase 2 the emissions were
16: Amounts of energy for sale (after own use) and of nutrients produced in the different scenarios, and allocation factors between energy and nutrients.

leading to highest CHP plant emissions and lowest with scenario 3. In addition, the N20 emissions from grasslands (S2) are higher than the climate emissions from additional slurry storage (S1) and covered storage (S3). These results are in line with Hijazi et al. (2016) review where it was observed that the composition of the feedstocks may affect the climate impacts for energy as well as the energy emissions decrease when moving towards societal and physical allocation methods towards economic and mass allocation methods.

Compared to an equivalent fossil energy option of natural gas production (almost 70g CO2eq per MJ, Ecoinvent), the results showed that emissions for anaerobic digestion allocated for energy has a significantly lower emissions in all three scenarios despite the allocation method used (Fig. 5). These results are in line with study results by Paolini et al., (2018), Hijazi et al., 2016, Winquist et al., (2015) and Rasi et al., (2012).

The largest share of climate emissions for energy (Fig. 6) in all scenarios with all allocation methods was generated by CHP plant including both emissions from producing (pre-storage, digestate separation, hygienisation, pumping of feedstocks in reactor and production of energy). However, in case of CHP plant, it is utilizing the self-produced energy so the emissions bought elsewhere are avoided and the total amount of emissions during anaerobic digestion process are targeted for the end-product energy (MJ) to be sold.

The second largest share of climate emissions for energy was produced during feedstock handling (slurry storage and co- feedstock procurement) with greater variation between scenarios due differences in co-feedstock handling practices (mixing and separation of slurry for solid fraction in S1, grass mowing from uncultivated lands in S2 and storage of side streams by the food industry at its place of origin in S1): N20 emissions from grasslands (S2) were the most significant, followed by slurry storage with floating cover and procurements (S1) and led to lowest emissions due covered storage in food industry and zero emissions during production (S3).

The transportation of feedstock (including pumping of feed- stocks to vehicle) had the lowest share of emissions during anaer- obic digestion (phase 1). Emissions from empty cargo was avoided when the semi-trailer truck transporting the slurry and grass to the biogas plant (included phase 1) was assumed to have returned to
Fig. 5. Climate emissions of biogas-based energy produced from different feedstocks using different allocation methods in comparison to using fossil energy.

Fig. 6. Climate emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for energy during feedstock handling, transport and CHP plant (phase 1) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and economic and mass allocation methods.
the same farms with the digested fractions (avoided in phase 1 and included in phase 2).

However, this is comparison between anaerobic digestion pro- cesses with significantly lower emissions compared to fossil en- ergy. A longer time of slurry storage in farms would emerge without digestion option: the slurry transportation every two weeks from the farm to the biogas plant decreased the farm storage time by 80%. The significantly smallest share of emissions was with transportation of feedstock.

The emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate (Fig. 7) are naturally zero when 100% of production is allocated to energy, but with economic allocation emissions the emissions levels varied between 1.1 and 1.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on the scenario (S1-S3). With mass allocation emissions higher, between 1.5 and 2.4kg CO2 eq/kg N. These results are in line with Hijazi et al. (2016) review observations where digestate emissions were seen to increase when moving towards societal and physical allocation methods towards economic and mass allocation methods. The scenarios with highest and lowest emissions for digestate was the
same as for energy: highest with scenario 2 (S2) and lowest with scenario 3 (S3) (see Fig. 8).

The results showed that with each of the allocation methods and scenarios the emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate has lower climate emissions than mineral fertiliser pro- duction (5.3 kg CO2eq/kg N, DNV GL, 2015), which is in line with the estimate made by Winquist et al. (2015).

When considering the emissions for anaerobic digestion allo- cated for digestate (phase 1), the largest share of climate emissions was generated during CHP processing in case with economic and mass allocation methods and with scenarios 1 and 3. In scenario 2, CHP emissions were the same as with feedstock handling (pro- curement, storage, mixing, separation) with both economic and mass allocation methods. This is maybe because S2 feedstock contains highest amount of N and the emissions in case with digestate are allocated per N and not per MJ as with energy emis- sions. The transportation of feedstock had lowest share of emis- sions with all scenarios.

3.2. Climate emissions of digestate utilization as fertiliser on field

During storage, transportation and use of digestate, the climate emissions are between 8.2 and 8.4kg CO2 eq/kg N in scenarios S1-S3. These emissions are assessed only for digestate and are not therefore effected by allocation methods and depending only on different co-feedstocks, nutrient contents and masses in scenarios S1-S3 (phase 2, Fig. 9).

The climate emissions in all scenarios S1-S3 - are higher than those of mineral fertiliser storage, transportation and use (6.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N, calculated with IPCC coefficients). This is because mineral nitrogen fertilisers do not generate emissions during storage but the digested fractions may lose nitrogen as evaporated ammonia (0.5 and 0.6kg CO2 eq/kg N) during storage. In addition,

transportation of the digestate from biogas plant to the storage and to the field cause more emissions (0.7kg CO2eq/kg N in all sce- narios) than the transportation of mineral fertilisers from industry to the farm and to the field (0.09 kg CO2 eq/kg N, calculated with IPCC coefficients), although the distance to the industry to the farm was much longer for the mineral fertilisers (200 km) than the distance from plant to the storage at farm (20 km) for the digestate. This is because the emissions decrease with increasing nutrient content of the material and the digestate has less nutrients and requires more transportation to the field to achieve the same fer- tilising effect as more concentrated mineral fertilisers. Nitrogen content of mineral fertilisers as a reference can vary considerably and therefore in this study the mineral fertilisation was assumed to be conducted with similar nutrient amount per hectare as in the
