However, this is comparison between anaerobic digestion pro- cesses with significantly lower emissions compared to fossil en- ergy. A longer time of slurry storage in farms would emerge without digestion option: the slurry transportation every two weeks from the farm to the biogas plant decreased the farm storage time by 80%. The significantly smallest share of emissions was with transportation of feedstock.

The emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate (Fig. 7) are naturally zero when 100% of production is allocated to energy, but with economic allocation emissions the emissions levels varied between 1.1 and 1.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on the scenario (S1-S3). With mass allocation emissions higher, between 1.5 and 2.4kg CO2 eq/kg N. These results are in line with Hijazi et al. (2016) review observations where digestate emissions were seen to increase when moving towards societal and physical allocation methods towards economic and mass allocation methods. The scenarios with highest and lowest emissions for digestate was the
same as for energy: highest with scenario 2 (S2) and lowest with scenario 3 (S3) (see Fig. 8).

The results showed that with each of the allocation methods and scenarios the emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate has lower climate emissions than mineral fertiliser pro- duction (5.3 kg CO2eq/kg N, DNV GL, 2015), which is in line with the estimate made by Winquist et al. (2015).

When considering the emissions for anaerobic digestion allo- cated for digestate (phase 1), the largest share of climate emissions was generated during CHP processing in case with economic and mass allocation methods and with scenarios 1 and 3. In scenario 2, CHP emissions were the same as with feedstock handling (pro- curement, storage, mixing, separation) with both economic and mass allocation methods. This is maybe because S2 feedstock contains highest amount of N and the emissions in case with digestate are allocated per N and not per MJ as with energy emis- sions. The transportation of feedstock had lowest share of emis- sions with all scenarios.
Fig. 7. Climate emissions of biogas-based digestate produced from different feedstocks using different allocation methods in comparison to mineral fertiliser production.

Fig. 8. Climate emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate during feedstock handling, transport and CHP plant (phase 1) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and economic and mass allocation methods.
3.2. Climate emissions of digestate utilization as fertiliser on field

During storage, transportation and use of digestate, the climate emissions are between 8.2 and 8.4kg CO2 eq/kg N in scenarios S1-S3. These emissions are assessed only for digestate and are not therefore effected by allocation methods and depending only on different co-feedstocks, nutrient contents and masses in scenarios S1-S3 (phase 2, Fig. 9).

The climate emissions in all scenarios S1-S3 - are higher than those of mineral fertiliser storage, transportation and use (6.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N, calculated with IPCC coefficients). This is because mineral nitrogen fertilisers do not generate emissions during storage but the digested fractions may lose nitrogen as evaporated ammonia (0.5 and 0.6kg CO2 eq/kg N) during storage. In addition,

transportation of the digestate from biogas plant to the storage and to the field cause more emissions (0.7kg CO2eq/kg N in all sce- narios) than the transportation of mineral fertilisers from industry to the farm and to the field (0.09 kg CO2 eq/kg N, calculated with IPCC coefficients), although the distance to the industry to the farm was much longer for the mineral fertilisers (200 km) than the distance from plant to the storage at farm (20 km) for the digestate. This is because the emissions decrease with increasing nutrient content of the material and the digestate has less nutrients and requires more transportation to the field to achieve the same fer- tilising effect as more concentrated mineral fertilisers. Nitrogen content of mineral fertilisers as a reference can vary considerably and therefore in this study the mineral fertilisation was assumed to be conducted with similar nutrient amount per hectare as in the

12||||| "10 8 6 4 2 0"||||| "10 8 6 4 2 0"|||||Transportation and use of mineral fertiliser "10 8 6 4 2 0"|||||S1: Phase 2 S2: Phase 2 S3: Phase 2 ||||| ||||| ||100% allocation to energy|Economic allocation|Mass allocation|

Fig. 9. Climate emissions of mineral fertiliser storage, transportation and usage compared to climate emissions of digestate storage, transportation and use (Phase 2) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and allocation methods.

digested fractions and nitrogen content was assumed to average of those used in Finland. In addition, the direct N20 emissions from organic fertilisers (4.7 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) are little bit higher than with mineral fertilizers (4.6 kg CO2eq/kg N). Also the indirect N20 emissions are higher with digestate (2.0 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) than with mineral fertilisers (1.5 kg CO2eq/kg N). The digested fractions could also be processed further to receive more concentrated products resembling mineral fertilisers (Tampio et al., 2016; Vaneechaute et al., 2017). This might be viable in larger, regional biogas plants, reducing emissions from manure fertiliser use and replacing mineral fertilisers. In Finland, it has been esti- mated that manure phosphorus could cover all phosphorus fertil- isation required by cereals and grass if the manure could partly be transported from intensive animal production areas to regions needing the nutrients (Ylivainio et al., 2014). Also farm-scale biogas plants may improve the farm's nutrient self-sufficiency via improving the solubility of nitrogen (Winquist et al., 2015) and recycling the farm's excess plant biomasses, such as the grass from uncultivated fields in this study.

The highest climate emission for digestate storage, trans- portation and field use (phase 2) was with scenario 1 using sepa- rated solid fraction of pig slurry as co-feedstock (8.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N). The emissions were little bit lower and the same with scenario 2 and scenario 3 (8.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N).

The total emissions (Fig. 10) for digestate are received by combining the allocated emissions of anaerobic digestion for digestate (phase 1) mentioned in previous chapter (3.1.), with storage, transportation and use (phase 2). When emission are allocated 100% for energy during phase 1 it is leading total emis- sions of digestate to be the same as viewed during phase 2 (be- tween 8.2 and 8.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N). However, with economic allocation total emissions for digestate rises between 9.4 and 9.9 kg CO2 eq/kg N, and with mass allocation method even more between 9.9 and 10.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on different co-feedstocks in scenarios (S1-S3). This is because the production emissions of mineral fertilisers are significantly higher than emissons from anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate, they compensating the higher emissions of digestate during storage, transportation and field use. Therefore, the total emissions of digestate with all
allocation methods and in all scenarios were lower than emissions for mineral fertiliser production, transportation and use (11.71 kg CO2 eq/kg N).

Comparing the scenarios with different co-feedstocks, the highest total emissions for digestate was with S1 (8.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N) and lowest with S2 (8.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N) when during anaerobic digestion (phase 1) 100% of emissions were allocated to energy. In case of economic allocation the highest total emissions were again with S1 (10.0 kg CO2 eq/kg N) but now the lowest turned out to be with S3 (9.3 kg CO2 eq/kg N) due smallest emission impact during anaerobic digestion (phase 1). In case of mass allocation the highest emissions were the same with scenario S1 and S2 (10.6 kg CO2 eq/ kg N) and lowest with S3 (9.7 kg CO2 eq/kg N) (Fig. 10).

The highest climate emissions came from fertiliser use of the digested fractions on the field (Fig. 11) ranging between 6.9 and 7.1 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending slightly on scenarios (S1-S3). The field use as one of the highest source for emissions has been perceived also by Rehl and Müller (2011), Rasi et al. (2012), Brockmann et al. (2014) and Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015). The dominant sources for emissions are direct N20 emissions (4.7 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) and the indirect N20 emissions (2.0 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) from organic fertiliser application on the field.

The next following emission sources did not show any more significant differences. The climate emissions of transportation during phase 2 was the same (0.7 kg CO2 eq/kg N) with all sce- narios (S1-S3) and emissions during digestate storage were a bit lower between 0.5 and 0.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending slightly on scenarios (S1-S3). In this study, covered storages were presently assumed to minimise the emissions from digestate use (Edelmann et al., 2001; Boulamanti et al., 2013; Mezzullo et al., 2013; Bachmaier et al., 2013) and open storage of digested residues is usually considered one of the biggest sources of emissions (Hijazi et al., 2016). In addition, the emissions during feedstock handling were between 0.0 and 1.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N and during CHP pro- cessing between 0.0 and 1.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on sce- narios (S1-S3) and allocation method. When we combined the emissions of transportation during phase 1 (0.0 and 0.1 kg CO2 eq/ kg N) with transportation during phase 2 the emissions were
19: Amounts of energy for sale (after own use) and of nutrients produced in the different scenarios, and allocation factors between energy and nutrients.

leading to highest CHP plant emissions and lowest with scenario 3. In addition, the N20 emissions from grasslands (S2) are higher than the climate emissions from additional slurry storage (S1) and covered storage (S3). These results are in line with Hijazi et al. (2016) review where it was observed that the composition of the feedstocks may affect the climate impacts for energy as well as the energy emissions decrease when moving towards societal and physical allocation methods towards economic and mass allocation methods.

Compared to an equivalent fossil energy option of natural gas production (almost 70g CO2eq per MJ, Ecoinvent), the results showed that emissions for anaerobic digestion allocated for energy has a significantly lower emissions in all three scenarios despite the allocation method used (Fig. 5). These results are in line with study results by Paolini et al., (2018), Hijazi et al., 2016, Winquist et al., (2015) and Rasi et al., (2012).

The largest share of climate emissions for energy (Fig. 6) in all scenarios with all allocation methods was generated by CHP plant including both emissions from producing (pre-storage, digestate separation, hygienisation, pumping of feedstocks in reactor and production of energy). However, in case of CHP plant, it is utilizing the self-produced energy so the emissions bought elsewhere are avoided and the total amount of emissions during anaerobic digestion process are targeted for the end-product energy (MJ) to be sold.

The second largest share of climate emissions for energy was produced during feedstock handling (slurry storage and co- feedstock procurement) with greater variation between scenarios due differences in co-feedstock handling practices (mixing and separation of slurry for solid fraction in S1, grass mowing from uncultivated lands in S2 and storage of side streams by the food industry at its place of origin in S1): N20 emissions from grasslands (S2) were the most significant, followed by slurry storage with floating cover and procurements (S1) and led to lowest emissions due covered storage in food industry and zero emissions during production (S3).

The transportation of feedstock (including pumping of feed- stocks to vehicle) had the lowest share of emissions during anaer- obic digestion (phase 1). The next following emission sources did not show any more significant differences. The climate emissions of transportation during phase 2 was the same (0.7 kg CO2 eq/kg N) with all sce- narios (S1-S3) and emissions during digestate storage were a bit lower between 0.5 and 0.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending slightly on scenarios (S1-S3). In this study, covered storages were presently assumed to minimise the emissions from digestate use (Edelmann et al., 2001; Boulamanti et al., 2013; Mezzullo et al., 2013; Bachmaier et al., 2013) and open storage of digested residues is usually considered one of the biggest sources of emissions (Hijazi et al., 2016). In addition, the emissions during feedstock handling were between 0.0 and 1.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N and during CHP pro- cessing between 0.0 and 1.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on sce- narios (S1-S3) and allocation method. When we combined the emissions of transportation during phase 1 (0.0 and 0.1 kg CO2 eq/ kg N) with transportation during phase 2 the emissions were
21: Amounts of energy for sale (after own use) and of nutrients produced in the different scenarios, and allocation factors between energy and nutrients.

leading to highest CHP plant emissions and lowest with scenario 3. In addition, the N20 emissions from grasslands (S2) are higher than the climate emissions from additional slurry storage (S1) and covered storage (S3). These results are in line with Hijazi et al. (2016) review where it was observed that the composition of the feedstocks may affect the climate impacts for energy as well as the energy emissions decrease when moving towards societal and physical allocation methods towards economic and mass allocation methods.

Compared to an equivalent fossil energy option of natural gas production (almost 70g CO2eq per MJ, Ecoinvent), the results showed that emissions for anaerobic digestion allocated for energy has a significantly lower emissions in all three scenarios despite the allocation method used (Fig. 5). These results are in line with study results by Paolini et al., (2018), Hijazi et al., 2016, Winquist et al., (2015) and Rasi et al., (2012).

The largest share of climate emissions for energy (Fig. 6) in all scenarios with all allocation methods was generated by CHP plant including both emissions from producing (pre-storage, digestate separation, hygienisation, pumping of feedstocks in reactor and production of energy). However, in case of CHP plant, it is utilizing the self-produced energy so the emissions bought elsewhere are avoided and the total amount of emissions during anaerobic digestion process are targeted for the end-product energy (MJ) to be sold.

The second largest share of climate emissions for energy was produced during feedstock handling (slurry storage and co- feedstock procurement) with greater variation between scenarios due differences in co-feedstock handling practices (mixing and separation of slurry for solid fraction in S1, grass mowing from uncultivated lands in S2 and storage of side streams by the food industry at its place of origin in S1): N20 emissions from grasslands (S2) were the most significant, followed by slurry storage with floating cover and procurements (S1) and led to lowest emissions due covered storage in food industry and zero emissions during production (S3).

The transportation of feedstock (including pumping of feed- stocks to vehicle) had the lowest share of emissions during anaer- obic digestion (phase 1). Emissions from empty cargo was avoided when the semi-trailer truck transporting the slurry and grass to the biogas plant (included phase 1) was assumed to have returned to
Fig. 5. Climate emissions of biogas-based energy produced from different feedstocks using different allocation methods in comparison to using fossil energy.

Fig. 6. Climate emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for energy during feedstock handling, transport and CHP plant (phase 1) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and economic and mass allocation methods.
the same farms with the digested fractions (avoided in phase 1 and included in phase 2).

However, this is comparison between anaerobic digestion pro- cesses with significantly lower emissions compared to fossil en- ergy. A longer time of slurry storage in farms would emerge without digestion option: the slurry transportation every two weeks from the farm to the biogas plant decreased the farm storage time by 80%. The significantly smallest share of emissions was with transportation of feedstock.

The emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate (Fig. 7) are naturally zero when 100% of production is allocated to energy, but with economic allocation emissions the emissions levels varied between 1.1 and 1.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on the scenario (S1-S3). With mass allocation emissions higher, between 1.5 and 2.4kg CO2 eq/kg N. These results are in line with Hijazi et al. (2016) review observations where digestate emissions were seen to increase when moving towards societal and physical allocation methods towards economic and mass allocation methods. The scenarios with highest and lowest emissions for digestate was the
same as for energy: highest with scenario 2 (S2) and lowest with scenario 3 (S3) (see Fig. 8).

The results showed that with each of the allocation methods and scenarios the emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate has lower climate emissions than mineral fertiliser pro- duction (5.3 kg CO2eq/kg N, DNV GL, 2015), which is in line with the estimate made by Winquist et al. (2015).

When considering the emissions for anaerobic digestion allo- cated for digestate (phase 1), the largest share of climate emissions was generated during CHP processing in case with economic and mass allocation methods and with scenarios 1 and 3. In scenario 2, CHP emissions were the same as with feedstock handling (pro- curement, storage, mixing, separation) with both economic and mass allocation methods. This is maybe because S2 feedstock contains highest amount of N and the emissions in case with digestate are allocated per N and not per MJ as with energy emis- sions. The transportation of feedstock had lowest share of emis- sions with all scenarios.
Fig. 7. Climate emissions of biogas-based digestate produced from different feedstocks using different allocation methods in comparison to mineral fertiliser production.

Fig. 8. Climate emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate during feedstock handling, transport and CHP plant (phase 1) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and economic and mass allocation methods.
3.2. Climate emissions of digestate utilization as fertiliser on field

During storage, transportation and use of digestate, the climate emissions are between 8.2 and 8.4kg CO2 eq/kg N in scenarios S1-S3. These emissions are assessed only for digestate and are not therefore effected by allocation methods and depending only on different co-feedstocks, nutrient contents and masses in scenarios S1-S3 (phase 2, Fig. 9).

The climate emissions in all scenarios S1-S3 - are higher than those of mineral fertiliser storage, transportation and use (6.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N, calculated with IPCC coefficients). This is because mineral nitrogen fertilisers do not generate emissions during storage but the digested fractions may lose nitrogen as evaporated ammonia (0.5 and 0.6kg CO2 eq/kg N) during storage. In addition,

transportation of the digestate from biogas plant to the storage and to the field cause more emissions (0.7kg CO2eq/kg N in all sce- narios) than the transportation of mineral fertilisers from industry to the farm and to the field (0.09 kg CO2 eq/kg N, calculated with IPCC coefficients), although the distance to the industry to the farm was much longer for the mineral fertilisers (200 km) than the distance from plant to the storage at farm (20 km) for the digestate. This is because the emissions decrease with increasing nutrient content of the material and the digestate has less nutrients and requires more transportation to the field to achieve the same fer- tilising effect as more concentrated mineral fertilisers. Nitrogen content of mineral fertilisers as a reference can vary considerably and therefore in this study the mineral fertilisation was assumed to be conducted with similar nutrient amount per hectare as in the

12||||| "10 8 6 4 2 0"||||| "10 8 6 4 2 0"|||||Transportation and use of mineral fertiliser "10 8 6 4 2 0"|||||S1: Phase 2 S2: Phase 2 S3: Phase 2 ||||| ||||| ||100% allocation to energy|Economic allocation|Mass allocation|

Fig. 9. Climate emissions of mineral fertiliser storage, transportation and usage compared to climate emissions of digestate storage, transportation and use (Phase 2) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and allocation methods.

digested fractions and nitrogen content was assumed to average of those used in Finland. In addition, the direct N20 emissions from organic fertilisers (4.7 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) are little bit higher than with mineral fertilizers (4.6 kg CO2eq/kg N). Also the indirect N20 emissions are higher with digestate (2.0 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) than with mineral fertilisers (1.5 kg CO2eq/kg N). The digested fractions could also be processed further to receive more concentrated products resembling mineral fertilisers (Tampio et al., 2016; Vaneechaute et al., 2017). This might be viable in larger, regional biogas plants, reducing emissions from manure fertiliser use and replacing mineral fertilisers. In Finland, it has been esti- mated that manure phosphorus could cover all phosphorus fertil- isation required by cereals and grass if the manure could partly be transported from intensive animal production areas to regions needing the nutrients (Ylivainio et al., 2014). Also farm-scale biogas plants may improve the farm's nutrient self-sufficiency via improving the solubility of nitrogen (Winquist et al., 2015) and recycling the farm's excess plant biomasses, such as the grass from uncultivated fields in this study.

The highest climate emission for digestate storage, trans- portation and field use (phase 2) was with scenario 1 using sepa- rated solid fraction of pig slurry as co-feedstock (8.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N). The emissions were little bit lower and the same with scenario 2 and scenario 3 (8.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N).

The total emissions (Fig. 10) for digestate are received by combining the allocated emissions of anaerobic digestion for digestate (phase 1) mentioned in previous chapter (3.1.), with storage, transportation and use (phase 2). When emission are allocated 100% for energy during phase 1 it is leading total emis- sions of digestate to be the same as viewed during phase 2 (be- tween 8.2 and 8.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N). However, with economic allocation total emissions for digestate rises between 9.4 and 9.9 kg CO2 eq/kg N, and with mass allocation method even more between 9.9 and 10.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on different co-feedstocks in scenarios (S1-S3). This is because the production emissions of mineral fertilisers are significantly higher than emissons from anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate, they compensating the higher emissions of digestate during storage, transportation and field use. Therefore, the total emissions of digestate with all
allocation methods and in all scenarios were lower than emissions for mineral fertiliser production, transportation and use (11.71 kg CO2 eq/kg N).

Comparing the scenarios with different co-feedstocks, the highest total emissions for digestate was with S1 (8.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N) and lowest with S2 (8.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N) when during anaerobic digestion (phase 1) 100% of emissions were allocated to energy. In case of economic allocation the highest total emissions were again with S1 (10.0 kg CO2 eq/kg N) but now the lowest turned out to be with S3 (9.3 kg CO2 eq/kg N) due smallest emission impact during anaerobic digestion (phase 1). In case of mass allocation the highest emissions were the same with scenario S1 and S2 (10.6 kg CO2 eq/ kg N) and lowest with S3 (9.7 kg CO2 eq/kg N) (Fig. 10).

In addition, the direct N20 emissions from organic fertilisers (4.7 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) are little bit higher than with mineral fertilizers (4.6 kg CO2eq/kg N). Also the indirect N20 emissions are higher with digestate (2.0 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) than with mineral fertilisers (1.5 kg CO2eq/kg N). The digested fractions could also be processed further to receive more concentrated products resembling mineral fertilisers (Tampio et al., 2016; Vaneechaute et al., 2017). This might be viable in larger, regional biogas plants, reducing emissions from manure fertiliser use and replacing mineral fertilisers. In Finland, it has been esti- mated that manure phosphorus could cover all phosphorus fertil- isation required by cereals and grass if the manure could partly be transported from intensive animal production areas to regions needing the nutrients (Ylivainio et al., 2014). Also farm-scale biogas plants may improve the farm's nutrient self-sufficiency via improving the solubility of nitrogen (Winquist et al., 2015) and recycling the farm's excess plant biomasses, such as the grass from uncultivated fields in this study.

The highest climate emission for digestate storage, trans- portation and field use (phase 2) was with scenario 1 using sepa- rated solid fraction of pig slurry as co-feedstock (8.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N). The emissions were little bit lower and the same with scenario 2 and scenario 3 (8.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N).

The total emissions (Fig. 10) for digestate are received by combining the allocated emissions of anaerobic digestion for digestate (phase 1) mentioned in previous chapter (3.1.), with storage, transportation and use (phase 2). When emission are allocated 100% for energy during phase 1 it is leading total emis- sions of digestate to be the same as viewed during phase 2 (be- tween 8.2 and 8.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N). However, with economic allocation total emissions for digestate rises between 9.4 and 9.9 kg CO2 eq/kg N, and with mass allocation method even more between 9.9 and 10.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on different co-feedstocks in scenarios (S1-S3). This is because the production emissions of mineral fertilisers are significantly higher than emissons from anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate, they compensating the higher emissions of digestate during storage, transportation and field use. Therefore, the total emissions of digestate with all
allocation methods and in all scenarios were lower than emissions for mineral fertiliser production, transportation and use (11.71 kg CO2 eq/kg N).

Comparing the scenarios with different co-feedstocks, the highest total emissions for digestate was with S1 (8.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N) and lowest with S2 (8.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N) when during anaerobic digestion (phase 1) 100% of emissions were allocated to energy. In case of economic allocation the highest total emissions were again with S1 (10.0 kg CO2 eq/kg N) but now the lowest turned out to be with S3 (9.3 kg CO2 eq/kg N) due smallest emission impact during anaerobic digestion (phase 1). In case of mass allocation the highest emissions were the same with scenario S1 and S2 (10.6 kg CO2 eq/ kg N) and lowest with S3 (9.7 kg CO2 eq/kg N) (Fig. 10).

The highest climate emissions came from fertiliser use of the digested fractions on the field (Fig. 11) ranging between 6.9 and 7.1 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending slightly on scenarios (S1-S3). The field use as one of the highest source for emissions has been perceived also by Rehl and Müller (2011), Rasi et al. (2012), Brockmann et al. (2014) and Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015). The dominant sources for emissions are direct N20 emissions (4.7 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) and the indirect N20 emissions (2.0 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) from organic fertiliser application on the field.

The next following emission sources did not show any more significant differences. The climate emissions of transportation during phase 2 was the same (0.7 kg CO2 eq/kg N) with all sce- narios (S1-S3) and emissions during digestate storage were a bit lower between 0.5 and 0.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending slightly on scenarios (S1-S3). In this study, covered storages were presently assumed to minimise the emissions from digestate use (Edelmann et al., 2001; Boulamanti et al., 2013; Mezzullo et al., 2013; Bachmaier et al., 2013) and open storage of digested residues is usually considered one of the biggest sources of emissions (Hijazi et al., 2016). In addition, the emissions during feedstock handling were between 0.0 and 1.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N and during CHP pro- cessing between 0.0 and 1.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on sce- narios (S1-S3) and allocation method. When we combined the emissions of transportation during phase 1 (0.0 and 0.1 kg CO2 eq/ kg N) with transportation during phase 2 the emissions were
24: Amounts of energy for sale (after own use) and of nutrients produced in the different scenarios, and allocation factors between energy and nutrients.

leading to highest CHP plant emissions and lowest with scenario 3. In addition, the N20 emissions from grasslands (S2) are higher than the climate emissions from additional slurry storage (S1) and covered storage (S3). These results are in line with Hijazi et al. (2016) review where it was observed that the composition of the feedstocks may affect the climate impacts for energy as well as the energy emissions decrease when moving towards societal and physical allocation methods towards economic and mass allocation methods.

Compared to an equivalent fossil energy option of natural gas production (almost 70g CO2eq per MJ, Ecoinvent), the results showed that emissions for anaerobic digestion allocated for energy has a significantly lower emissions in all three scenarios despite the allocation method used (Fig. 5). These results are in line with study results by Paolini et al., (2018), Hijazi et al., 2016, Winquist et al., (2015) and Rasi et al., (2012).

The largest share of climate emissions for energy (Fig. 6) in all scenarios with all allocation methods was generated by CHP plant including both emissions from producing (pre-storage, digestate separation, hygienisation, pumping of feedstocks in reactor and production of energy). However, in case of CHP plant, it is utilizing the self-produced energy so the emissions bought elsewhere are avoided and the total amount of emissions during anaerobic digestion process are targeted for the end-product energy (MJ) to be sold.

The second largest share of climate emissions for energy was produced during feedstock handling (slurry storage and co- feedstock procurement) with greater variation between scenarios due differences in co-feedstock handling practices (mixing and separation of slurry for solid fraction in S1, grass mowing from uncultivated lands in S2 and storage of side streams by the food industry at its place of origin in S1): N20 emissions from grasslands (S2) were the most significant, followed by slurry storage with floating cover and procurements (S1) and led to lowest emissions due covered storage in food industry and zero emissions during production (S3).

The transportation of feedstock (including pumping of feed- stocks to vehicle) had the lowest share of emissions during anaer- obic digestion (phase 1). Emissions from empty cargo was avoided when the semi-trailer truck transporting the slurry and grass to the biogas plant (included phase 1) was assumed to have returned to
Fig. 5. Climate emissions of biogas-based energy produced from different feedstocks using different allocation methods in comparison to using fossil energy.

Fig. 6. Climate emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for energy during feedstock handling, transport and CHP plant (phase 1) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and economic and mass allocation methods.
the same farms with the digested fractions (avoided in phase 1 and included in phase 2).

However, this is comparison between anaerobic digestion pro- cesses with significantly lower emissions compared to fossil en- ergy. A longer time of slurry storage in farms would emerge without digestion option: the slurry transportation every two weeks from the farm to the biogas plant decreased the farm storage time by 80%. The significantly smallest share of emissions was with transportation of feedstock.

The emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate (Fig. 7) are naturally zero when 100% of production is allocated to energy, but with economic allocation emissions the emissions levels varied between 1.1 and 1.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on the scenario (S1-S3). With mass allocation emissions higher, between 1.5 and 2.4kg CO2 eq/kg N. These results are in line with Hijazi et al. (2016) review observations where digestate emissions were seen to increase when moving towards societal and physical allocation methods towards economic and mass allocation methods. The scenarios with highest and lowest emissions for digestate was the
same as for energy: highest with scenario 2 (S2) and lowest with scenario 3 (S3) (see Fig. 8).

The results showed that with each of the allocation methods and scenarios the emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate has lower climate emissions than mineral fertiliser pro- duction (5.3 kg CO2eq/kg N, DNV GL, 2015), which is in line with the estimate made by Winquist et al. (2015).

When considering the emissions for anaerobic digestion allo- cated for digestate (phase 1), the largest share of climate emissions was generated during CHP processing in case with economic and mass allocation methods and with scenarios 1 and 3. In scenario 2, CHP emissions were the same as with feedstock handling (pro- curement, storage, mixing, separation) with both economic and mass allocation methods. This is maybe because S2 feedstock contains highest amount of N and the emissions in case with digestate are allocated per N and not per MJ as with energy emis- sions. The transportation of feedstock had lowest share of emis- sions with all scenarios.

Fig. 8. Climate emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate during feedstock handling, transport and CHP plant (phase 1) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and economic and mass allocation methods.
3.2. Climate emissions of digestate utilization as fertiliser on field

During storage, transportation and use of digestate, the climate emissions are between 8.2 and 8.4kg CO2 eq/kg N in scenarios S1-S3. These emissions are assessed only for digestate and are not therefore effected by allocation methods and depending only on different co-feedstocks, nutrient contents and masses in scenarios S1-S3 (phase 2, Fig. 9).

The climate emissions in all scenarios S1-S3 - are higher than those of mineral fertiliser storage, transportation and use (6.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N, calculated with IPCC coefficients). This is because mineral nitrogen fertilisers do not generate emissions during storage but the digested fractions may lose nitrogen as evaporated ammonia (0.5 and 0.6kg CO2 eq/kg N) during storage. In addition,

transportation of the digestate from biogas plant to the storage and to the field cause more emissions (0.7kg CO2eq/kg N in all sce- narios) than the transportation of mineral fertilisers from industry to the farm and to the field (0.09 kg CO2 eq/kg N, calculated with IPCC coefficients), although the distance to the industry to the farm was much longer for the mineral fertilisers (200 km) than the distance from plant to the storage at farm (20 km) for the digestate. This is because the emissions decrease with increasing nutrient content of the material and the digestate has less nutrients and requires more transportation to the field to achieve the same fer- tilising effect as more concentrated mineral fertilisers. Nitrogen content of mineral fertilisers as a reference can vary considerably and therefore in this study the mineral fertilisation was assumed to be conducted with similar nutrient amount per hectare as in the

(2016) review observations where digestate emissions were seen to increase when moving towards societal and physical allocation methods towards economic and mass allocation methods. The scenarios with highest and lowest emissions for digestate was the
same as for energy: highest with scenario 2 (S2) and lowest with scenario 3 (S3) (see Fig. 8).

The results showed that with each of the allocation methods and scenarios the emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate has lower climate emissions than mineral fertiliser pro- duction (5.3 kg CO2eq/kg N, DNV GL, 2015), which is in line with the estimate made by Winquist et al. (2015).

When considering the emissions for anaerobic digestion allo- cated for digestate (phase 1), the largest share of climate emissions was generated during CHP processing in case with economic and mass allocation methods and with scenarios 1 and 3. In scenario 2, CHP emissions were the same as with feedstock handling (pro- curement, storage, mixing, separation) with both economic and mass allocation methods. This is maybe because S2 feedstock contains highest amount of N and the emissions in case with digestate are allocated per N and not per MJ as with energy emis- sions. The transportation of feedstock had lowest share of emis- sions with all scenarios.
Fig. 7. Climate emissions of biogas-based digestate produced from different feedstocks using different allocation methods in comparison to mineral fertiliser production.

Fig. 8. Climate emissions of anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate during feedstock handling, transport and CHP plant (phase 1) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and economic and mass allocation methods.
3.2. Climate emissions of digestate utilization as fertiliser on field

During storage, transportation and use of digestate, the climate emissions are between 8.2 and 8.4kg CO2 eq/kg N in scenarios S1-S3. These emissions are assessed only for digestate and are not therefore effected by allocation methods and depending only on different co-feedstocks, nutrient contents and masses in scenarios S1-S3 (phase 2, Fig. 9).

The climate emissions in all scenarios S1-S3 - are higher than those of mineral fertiliser storage, transportation and use (6.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N, calculated with IPCC coefficients). This is because mineral nitrogen fertilisers do not generate emissions during storage but the digested fractions may lose nitrogen as evaporated ammonia (0.5 and 0.6kg CO2 eq/kg N) during storage. In addition,

transportation of the digestate from biogas plant to the storage and to the field cause more emissions (0.7kg CO2eq/kg N in all sce- narios) than the transportation of mineral fertilisers from industry to the farm and to the field (0.09 kg CO2 eq/kg N, calculated with IPCC coefficients), although the distance to the industry to the farm was much longer for the mineral fertilisers (200 km) than the distance from plant to the storage at farm (20 km) for the digestate. This is because the emissions decrease with increasing nutrient content of the material and the digestate has less nutrients and requires more transportation to the field to achieve the same fer- tilising effect as more concentrated mineral fertilisers. Nitrogen content of mineral fertilisers as a reference can vary considerably and therefore in this study the mineral fertilisation was assumed to be conducted with similar nutrient amount per hectare as in the

Fig. 9. Climate emissions of mineral fertiliser storage, transportation and usage compared to climate emissions of digestate storage, transportation and use (Phase 2) with different raw materials (S1-S3) and allocation methods.

digested fractions and nitrogen content was assumed to average of those used in Finland. In addition, the direct N20 emissions from organic fertilisers (4.7 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) are little bit higher than with mineral fertilizers (4.6 kg CO2eq/kg N). Also the indirect N20 emissions are higher with digestate (2.0 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) than with mineral fertilisers (1.5 kg CO2eq/kg N). The digested fractions could also be processed further to receive more concentrated products resembling mineral fertilisers (Tampio et al., 2016; Vaneechaute et al., 2017). This might be viable in larger, regional biogas plants, reducing emissions from manure fertiliser use and replacing mineral fertilisers. In Finland, it has been esti- mated that manure phosphorus could cover all phosphorus fertil- isation required by cereals and grass if the manure could partly be transported from intensive animal production areas to regions needing the nutrients (Ylivainio et al., 2014). Also farm-scale biogas plants may improve the farm's nutrient self-sufficiency via improving the solubility of nitrogen (Winquist et al., 2015) and recycling the farm's excess plant biomasses, such as the grass from uncultivated fields in this study.

The highest climate emission for digestate storage, trans- portation and field use (phase 2) was with scenario 1 using sepa- rated solid fraction of pig slurry as co-feedstock (8.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N). The emissions were little bit lower and the same with scenario 2 and scenario 3 (8.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N).

The total emissions (Fig. 10) for digestate are received by combining the allocated emissions of anaerobic digestion for digestate (phase 1) mentioned in previous chapter (3.1.), with storage, transportation and use (phase 2). When emission are allocated 100% for energy during phase 1 it is leading total emis- sions of digestate to be the same as viewed during phase 2 (be- tween 8.2 and 8.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N). However, with economic allocation total emissions for digestate rises between 9.4 and 9.9 kg CO2 eq/kg N, and with mass allocation method even more between 9.9 and 10.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on different co-feedstocks in scenarios (S1-S3). This is because the production emissions of mineral fertilisers are significantly higher than emissons from anaerobic digestion allocated for digestate, they compensating the higher emissions of digestate during storage, transportation and field use. Therefore, the total emissions of digestate with all
allocation methods and in all scenarios were lower than emissions for mineral fertiliser production, transportation and use (11.71 kg CO2 eq/kg N).

Comparing the scenarios with different co-feedstocks, the highest total emissions for digestate was with S1 (8.4 kg CO2 eq/kg N) and lowest with S2 (8.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N) when during anaerobic digestion (phase 1) 100% of emissions were allocated to energy. In case of economic allocation the highest total emissions were again with S1 (10.0 kg CO2 eq/kg N) but now the lowest turned out to be with S3 (9.3 kg CO2 eq/kg N) due smallest emission impact during anaerobic digestion (phase 1). In case of mass allocation the highest emissions were the same with scenario S1 and S2 (10.6 kg CO2 eq/ kg N) and lowest with S3 (9.7 kg CO2 eq/kg N) (Fig. 10).

The highest climate emissions came from fertiliser use of the digested fractions on the field (Fig. 11) ranging between 6.9 and 7.1 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending slightly on scenarios (S1-S3). The field use as one of the highest source for emissions has been perceived also by Rehl and Müller (2011), Rasi et al. (2012), Brockmann et al. (2014) and Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015). The dominant sources for emissions are direct N20 emissions (4.7 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) and the indirect N20 emissions (2.0 kg CO2eq/kg N in all scenarios) from organic fertiliser application on the field.

The next following emission sources did not show any more significant differences. The climate emissions of transportation during phase 2 was the same (0.7 kg CO2 eq/kg N) with all sce- narios (S1-S3) and emissions during digestate storage were a bit lower between 0.5 and 0.6 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending slightly on scenarios (S1-S3). In this study, covered storages were presently assumed to minimise the emissions from digestate use (Edelmann et al., 2001; Boulamanti et al., 2013; Mezzullo et al., 2013; Bachmaier et al., 2013) and open storage of digested residues is usually considered one of the biggest sources of emissions (Hijazi et al., 2016). In addition, the emissions during feedstock handling were between 0.0 and 1.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N and during CHP pro- cessing between 0.0 and 1.2 kg CO2 eq/kg N depending on sce- narios (S1-S3) and allocation method. When we combined the emissions of transportation during phase 1 (0.0 and 0.1 kg CO2 eq/ kg N) with transportation during phase 2 the emissions were

ranging between 0.7 and 0.9 kg CO2 eq/kg N. The emissions during phase 2 were higher due more empty cargos not avoided (the dump truck returning to the biogas plant after transporting the solid fraction to the farm storage, and the tractor returning to the farm after spreading the digestate in the field). In addition, during phase 2 the transportation distances were all together longer for higher transported masses of nutrients.

There is uncertainty of many values used in fuel consumption and other estimates. This research has focused only on climate emissions but, for example in decision making processes, other environmental impact categories must also be taken into account. For example, alongside with decreasing GHG emissions there is a risk for increasing ammonia emissions (Hou et al., 2015), between different actions and even within one biomass management chain.

2: In all the anaerobic digestion scenarios examined in this study, the climate emissions for energy decreased when the digestate was also considered and allocation was made on basis of economy or mass (100% allocated for energy > economic allocation > mass allocation). Vice versa, the climate emissions for digestate increased from zero when allocated on basis of economy or mass (100% allocated for energy < economic allocation < mass allocation).

With all the allocation methods, the climate emissions of raw material procurement and anaerobic digestion for energy and digestate were lower than that of fossil energy and production of mineral fertilisers. The highest level of climate emissions was with CHP plant and slurry storage which underline the importance of

emission abatement methods considering CHP plant and slurry storage. The scenario with the highest climate emissions for raw material procurement and anaerobic digestion process was with the one digesting pig slurry and grass from uncultivated fields due prominent N20 emissions from grasslands. The lowest level of emissions was with the scenario involving the digestation of pig slurry and side streams from the food industry due to avoided emissions from co-feedstock procurement (covered storage and zero emissions for the production of food industry sludge as waste).

During digestate storage, transportation and field use the emission were assessed only for digestate and not for energy so no allocation method was needed. The emissions were significantly higher than during raw material procurement and anaerobic digestion process and were estimated to have higher emissions than storage, transportation and use of mineral fertilisers. The source with significantly highest level of emissions was digestate field use. The scenario with highest emissions were with co- digestion of pig slurry and separated solid fraction of pig slurry.

However, when combining the emissions of raw material pro- curement and anaerobic digestion process allocated for digestate with the storage, transportation and use on the field, the total emissions were lower than those of mineral fertiliser production and field use. The total emissions were the highest with mass allocation method and lowest with scenarios where emissions during anaerobic digestion were allocated only to energy. However, the highest emissions varied little between S1 and S2 depending on allocation methods as well as on the co-feedstocks which balanced the differences between the emissions during anaerobic digestion and digestate field use leading to almost similar total emissions between different scenarios.

The relationship between climate emissions and farm's scale may follow a liner relationship because larger plants and field area have higher emissions (higher amount of emissions are targeted for higher amount of produced energy i.e. the ratio and the emissions per MJ and kg N may stay the same). However, since we have not studied this effect yet, it is safer conclude that the study results may be limited to the standard case situation only.
